text: Over eighteen years of democratic rule in South Africa, there appears to be a mood of retrenchment and diffidence regarding what constitutes the identity, dynamics and influence of the church in the public domain. This happens at a time when corruption appears to threaten any form of sustainable development, and Reformed churches in South Africa are, arguably, overdue a critical self-examination of their long-standing heritage of prophetic activity in the context of the endogenous struggle to eradicate corruption and achieve inclusive development. This article examines, with particular reference to the dynamic between the private and public spheres, possible models that may assist in promoting a clearer definition of the church's role in the public domain, particularly in respect of the Three frameworks that appear in the literature — namely, the Two Kingdoms model, the Neo-Calvinist approach and the Communicative Rationality approach.
headline: Fully Human Written
